perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall use all commercially reasonable efforts to secure distribution of the Wireless Products through CSPs and to ensure that the Wireless Products are Wireless Content License Agreement Multiple Properties / Sorrent, Inc. / Final PLZ ***** The omitted portions of this exhibit have been filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission pursuant to a request for confidential treatment under Rule 406 promulgated under the Securities Act of 1933. -6- Source: GLU MOBILE INC, S-1/A, 3/19/2007 available on substantially all medium and high volume handsets of all Licensed CSPs, which for the purposes of this obligation shall include VGSL.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall endeavor in good faith to seek distribution of the Wireless Products through CSPs and to ensure that the Wireless Products are Wireless Content License Agreement Multiple Properties / Sorrent, Inc. / Final PLZ ***** The omitted portions of this exhibit have been filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission pursuant to a request for confidential treatment under Rule 406 promulgated under the Securities Act of 1933. -6- Source: GLU MOBILE INC, S-1/A, 3/19/2007 available on many medium and high volume handsets of some Licensed CSPs; VGSL may or may not be included.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all commercially reasonable efforts' to 'endeavor in good faith' weakens the obligation. Also, substituting 'substantially all' with 'many' creates ambiguity, and the caveat about VGSL being optional further reduces the commitment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By weakening the language regarding distribution efforts and availability, the modified text introduces ambiguity about the level of effort required. This could be interpreted as a breach of the implied covenant if Licensee's efforts are deemed inadequate, even if technically compliant with the vague language.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessi
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'all commercially reasonable efforts' for distribution, which is a relatively high standard. The changed contract uses 'endeavor in good faith,' a weaker commitment. Moreover, the original language specifies 'substantially all medium and high volume handsets of all Licensed CSPs,' while the changed language uses 'many medium and high volume handsets of some Licensed CSPs,' further reducing the commitment and creating ambiguity. The Law Explanation correctly states that this weaker language 'introduces ambiguity about the level of effort required.' This ambiguity weakens the enforceability of the distribution obligation and could be construed as not acting in good faith, thus contradicting implied contractual obligations. Snippet 1 defines agreement broadly but does not directly address the issue of ambiguous obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee agrees to place on Licensee's website and in the Wireless Products, and on the packaging and related documentation, if any, and Marketing Materials for the Wireless Products, all trademarks, copyright notices, logos and other legal or proprietary designations of Fox in the form and manner reasonably directed by Fox, including, without limitation, a notice indicating that the Wireless Products are produced by Licensee under license from Fox.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee will consider including trademarks, copyright notices, logos, and other designations of Fox on its website and in the Wireless Products, as well as on the packaging and marketing materials, if feasible and as resources permit. The inclusion and manner of display of a notice indicating that the Wireless Products are produced by Licensee under license from Fox will be discussed in good faith.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a clear obligation. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'agrees to place' with weaker phrasing like 'will consider including' and 'if feasible and as resources permit.' The phrase 'discussed in good faith' doesn't guarantee placement, further diluting the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act protects trademarks. The original clause ensured proper attribution and notice, reducing the risk of consumer confusion. The modified text creates ambiguity about whether the marks will actually be displayed, potentially leading to consumer confusion and weakening Fox's trademark protection, which can be seen as contributory infringement if Licensee's actions enable others to infringe on Fox's trademarks.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Lanham Act by creating ambiguity around trademark usage, potentially leading to consumer confusion and weakening trademark protection. The original contract mandated the placement of trademarks, logos, and copyright notices, ensuring proper attribution. The changed text replaces this obligation with 'will consider including' and 'if feasible and as resources permit,' making the inclusion discretionary rather than mandatory. This directly undermines the Lanham Act's purpose of preventing consumer confusion, as stated in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which highlights the liability of any person who uses a reproduction of a registered mark in a way that is likely to cause confusion, mistake, or deception (15 U.S. Code § 1114). By making trademark inclusion optional, the revised text increases the risk of infringement.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall comply with all laws, rules, treaties, and regulations governing the distribution of the Wireless Products throughout the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee will endeavor to be mindful of applicable laws, rules, treaties, and regulations relevant to the distribution of Wireless Products within the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a clear, direct obligation to comply with all relevant laws. The modification replaces 'shall comply' with 'will endeavor to be mindful,' which is significantly weaker and more ambiguous. It suggests an awareness of the laws but does not mandate adherence.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17200 prohibits unfair competition, including unlawful business practices. By weakening the compliance obligation, the modified text creates ambiguity that could allow Licensee to engage in practices that, while technically 'mindful' of the law, may still be unlawful or unfair, thus violating this section. A simple awareness of the law is not enough, businesses must actively comply with the applicable laws, and regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall comply' with 'will endeavor to be mindful,' weakening the obligation to adhere to laws. This directly contradicts the prohibition of 'unfair competition' under California Code BPC 17200, which includes 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By only requiring the Licensee to be 'mindful' of the law, the contract allows for potential non-compliance and unfair practices. The original text imposed a strict obligation, whereas the changed text does not mandate adherence, potentially enabling practices that, while aware of the law, may still violate it.
